title: Lexicographically Smallest String After Adjacent Removals
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals for the recommended and fastest-runtime variants. The minimum-footprint C variant is implemented in a sibling C folder.

variants.languageReason: The constraint n <= 250 favors dense interval DP and compact lexicographic suffix state. C++ provides native bitsets, efficient string comparison, and the exact LeetCode class signature without VM, interpreter, or GC overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It gives the best balance of low-level bit operations, readable suffix DP, and mature LeetCode support.
Excellent for the memory objective, but manual result allocation and linked-state comparison make it less suitable for the readable default proposal.
Native and safe, but dense mutable interval tables and linked comparison state are more verbose and unlikely to improve LeetCode constants here.
Compiled, but slice bounds checks and runtime overhead add no benefit for this small dense DP.
Possible with arrays and strings, but managed-runtime overhead and object layout are less attractive than C++ for top-percentile memory and runtime.
Likely acceptable at n <= 250 with careful DP, but interpreter or VM overhead is avoidable and weaker for a top 1% performance target.

variants.approachJustification: Build a bitset interval table of fully removable substrings using endpoint enclosure and split concatenation. Then compute best[i] as the lexicographically smallest string obtainable from suffix i by choosing a kept character j after a removable prefix.

variants.languageReason: The runtime variant is dominated by fixed-size mask intersections and dense table lookups. C++ enables 64-bit row masks, byte comparison tables, and direct character indexing with minimal overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. Fixed arrays and inline helpers give tight native loops while preserving the LeetCode string-returning class interface.
Could match bitmask loops, but manual allocation and bookkeeping are better reserved for the memory variant; C++ is just as fast for the runtime table layout.
Native speed is possible, but bounds checks and ownership around dense mutable tables make it less likely to beat the C++ implementation.
Bounds checks and runtime baseline work against the O(n^2) hot path of table filling.
Primitive arrays can be competitive after JIT warmup, but C++ avoids VM startup, GC metadata, and range-check overhead.
The low-level bitmask and byte-table strategy is dominated by native compiled code for raw runtime.

variants.approachJustification: Use fixed 64-bit masks for removable intervals, then represent each answer as a linked choice. Maintain less[a][b] so candidate suffixes can be compared in O(1), avoiding intermediate strings.

reasoningSummary: A substring can be fully removed iff it is either enclosed by a consecutive endpoint pair around a removable inside, or split into two adjacent removable substrings. After this interval DP, the lexicographically smallest suffix answer chooses the next kept character s[j] only when the prefix before it is removable; the empty answer wins whenever the whole suffix is removable.
